tweets.text:
The @IEA reckons that UAE reached ~85% of pre-war oil exports in early June (ie, before the MoU was signed) thanks to a combination of bypass pipeline, Fujairah strategic storage and, importantly, dark SoH crossing along the Omani coastline. 

In early June, IEA puts UAE total oil exports (crude and condensates) at 4.3m b/d, up from 1.9m b/d in March. 

The chart below is IEA latest view from the Persian Gulf (June is data up to June 15th), showing the recovery, with UAE playing a key role (and obviously KSA via bypass pipeline).

tweets.text:
Sizable buyer put in a "market on open" trade recently. Normally this would have traded way off NAV with folks like Jane Street benefitting.

But we pay out of our pocket for a DMM who noticed the poor execution and delayed the open to ensure it was filled at a good price.

tweets.text:
I find it interesting that the degens on $RDDT are starting a viral campaign to save Wendy’s ( $WEN ).

The US fast food burger chain.

And the stock price is now up 20% overnight. 

Just for background: Wendy’s is a popular community meme, where people work behind the dumpsters after their portfolios go to 0 from 0DTE options.

(No positions, just found it amusing) 

I wonder if it’s going to work?

tweets.text:
The first @1000xPod was almost 4 years ago now. Crazy to look back and see what is effectively a time capsule of my life and markets. Had better hair back then. 

What started as a hobby while I was running a hedge fund is now turning into a real passion of mine. I love creating media and sharing ideas with all of you, and I working hard to expand the show. 

I think there is an important missing niche in financial media in between journalists and influencers. 

Both Jonah and I have managed over $1b at various points in time, and we come to you as people who have worked in the trenches, made and lost hundreds in a millions in a day -- and now want to show you how to do it as well. 

Our first episode ever linked below. Check it out just for fun, and hopefully stick with us on the journey to build a better financial media. 

https://t.co/K8LAphyZQx
tweets.full_text:
The first @1000xPod was almost 4 years ago now. Crazy to look back and see what is effectively a time capsule of my life and markets. Had better hair back then. 

What started as a hobby while I was running a hedge fund is now turning into a real passion of mine. I love creating media and sharing ideas with all of you, and I working hard to expand the show. 

I think there is an important missing niche in financial media in between journalists and influencers. 

Both Jonah and I have managed over $1b at various points in time, and we come to you as people who have worked in the trenches, made and lost hundreds in a millions in a day -- and now want to show you how to do it as well. 

Our first episode ever linked below. Check it out just for fun, and hopefully stick with us on the journey to build a better financial media. 

https://t.co/K8LAphyZQx

tweets.text:
This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the other human activity org-wide. Once you do all of the under the hood engineering work to make this "just work" (e.g. across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, security, etc.), Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way - you can talk to it as you would talk to a person and it can help with a very large variety of workloads.

Imo this is the 3rd major redesign of LLM UIUX. The first paradigm was that the LLM is a website you go to, the second was that it is an app you download to your computer. This third one is that it is a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans. It really takes a while to wrap your head around it, but it works and it is awesome.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Introducing Claude Tag, a new way for teams to work with Claude.

In Slack, Claude joins as a team member with access to the channels and tools you choose. Tag Claude in and delegate tasks to it while you focus on other work. https://t.co/R2C6A5Kcye

This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the other human activity org-wide. Once you do all of the under the hood engineering work to make this "just work" (e.g. across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, security, etc.), Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way - you can talk to it as you would talk to a person and it can help with a very large variety of workloads.

Imo this is the 3rd major redesign of LLM UIUX. The first paradigm was that the LLM is a website you go to, the second was that it is an app you download to your computer. This third one is that it is a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans. It really takes a while to wrap your head around it, but it works and it is awesome.

thread_tweets.text: The basic idea is easy and v0 is a hackathon project. The product here is a lot closer to *it actually works*, for enterprise grade deployments, and after quite a bit of internal experimentation and iteration. It’s kind of hard to describe other than (per the post) it’s writing majority of code, it’s deeply integrated, multiplayer, and it starts to feel like everyone is a manager. So I understand it looks easy to dismiss on quick reading but it’s not some LLM Q&A with RAG over Slack, it’s not even OpenClaw adjacent, it’s a different way of working entirely, for people and teams. I work from Slack now.
thread_tweets.full_text: The basic idea is easy and v0 is a hackathon project. The product here is a lot closer to *it actually works*, for enterprise grade deployments, and after quite a bit of internal experimentation and iteration. It’s kind of hard to describe other than (per the post) it’s writing majority of code, it’s deeply integrated, multiplayer, and it starts to feel like everyone is a manager. So I understand it looks easy to dismiss on quick reading but it’s not some LLM Q&A with RAG over Slack, it’s not even OpenClaw adjacent, it’s a different way of working entirely, for people and teams. I work from Slack now.

tweets.full_text:
This is a new paradigm for interacting with Claude that is significantly more "inline" with all the other human activity org-wide. Once you do all of the under the hood engineering work to make this "just work" (e.g. across tools, integrations, compute environments, memory, security, etc.), Claude basically joins the team in a seamless way - you can talk to it as you would talk to a person and it can help with a very large variety of workloads.

Imo this is the 3rd major redesign of LLM UIUX. The first paradigm was that the LLM is a website you go to, the second was that it is an app you download to your computer. This third one is that it is a self-contained, persistent, asynchronous entity with org-wide tools and context, working alongside teams of humans. It really takes a while to wrap your head around it, but it works and it is awesome.

The basic idea is easy and v0 is a hackathon project. The product here is a lot closer to *it actually works*, for enterprise grade deployments, and after quite a bit of internal experimentation and iteration. It’s kind of hard to describe other than (per the post) it’s writing majority of code, it’s deeply integrated, multiplayer, and it starts to feel like everyone is a manager. So I understand it looks easy to dismiss on quick reading but it’s not some LLM Q&A with RAG over Slack, it’s not even OpenClaw adjacent, it’s a different way of working entirely, for people and teams. I work from Slack now.

tweets.text:
this is the chatgpt moment for biology i’ve been waiting for

you can now effectively engineer a new protein, piece of DNA or RNA using this new AI-powered programming language,

 how it works:

-> it’s a programming language sat on top of 120 tools including ai models like Alphafold 3

-> you explain in plain english what you want to design. 

-> the system simulates a gene-evolution process through 4 primitives: sequencing the dna , rating the dna, generating novel mutations & then looping the sequence until you make a breakthrough!

they trialled this on sections of DNA and achieved a 32% success rate (3.5x better than standard)

can you imagine strapping fable 5 into this thing?

think we see a breakthrough such as 
a novel gene editing therapy discovered by AI by end of year
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Imagine programming protein, DNA, and RNA systems like you would write computer code, or even by natural language prompting of an AI agent. @brianhie and team just made this a reality with Proto: a high-level programming language for generative biology. https://t.co/1vwDutPUTJ

this is the chatgpt moment for biology i’ve been waiting for

you can now effectively engineer a new protein, piece of DNA or RNA using this new AI-powered programming language,

 how it works:

-> it’s a programming language sat on top of 120 tools including ai models like Alphafold 3

-> you explain in plain english what you want to design. 

-> the system simulates a gene-evolution process through 4 primitives: sequencing the dna , rating the dna, generating novel mutations & then looping the sequence until you make a breakthrough!

they trialled this on sections of DNA and achieved a 32% success rate (3.5x better than standard)

can you imagine strapping fable 5 into this thing?

think we see a breakthrough such as 
a novel gene editing therapy discovered by AI by end of year

tweets.text:
i just don’t buy the idea anthropic and openai are just going to sit around while chinese models out-compete them on cost

they’re obviously going to distill their flagship model into a much (much) cheaper one.

the best part about owning the smartest models is you can get them to build a cheaper one :)

watch out for the sonnet 5 release this week

tweets.text:
btw if claude sonnet 5 drops this week (i think it will) after gpt 5.6 got delayed - it’s a strong sign anthropic has built a self-improving model loop and we can expect to see rapid model releases going forwards

this is the golden era. models that build the next versions of themselves with minimal-to-zero humans in the loop. the barrier becomes compute.

mythos has obviously struck a chord. google has also delayed next major model upgrade while they figure out how to catch up to mythos standard 

talent is bleeding to consolidate to either openai or anthropic 

it’s not just researchers either, top economists are leaving their stuffy offices to join the ranks.

definitely a turning point.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
i wonder how much of the gpt 5.6 delay is because of government fable 5 ban.

model labs are increasingly being forced to keep new models internal otherwise risk customer dissatisfaction from a national ban. 

makes sense to sit tight until they know the safeguards needed and in

tweets.text:
its blowing my mind that 65% of product code at anthropic is now written by *tagging claude* in group chats of staff discussing what they want to build.

RIP the days of tediously writing long product docs, now you can literally go from slack to a production ready feature

claude code is barely 1+ years old btw
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: Claude Tag is an evolution of Claude Code, made more proactive and built to work with a full team. It’s now one of the main ways we get things done at Anthropic: 65% of our product team's code now comes from our internal version.
